entryTemplate.title: Short customer-facing title
entryTemplate.summary: A clear paragraph: what changed and why readers should care.
entryTemplate.detail: Optional. Extra paragraphs separated by blank lines (\n\n). No commits or ticket IDs.

entries.title: brief's Pulse: tiny trend lines, change-since-last read, and a fuller "what to watch" list
entries.summary: The six Pulse score cards now carry a slim sparkline so you can see direction at a glance, plus a small badge that says how many points each pillar moved since the last time the grid refreshed — helpful when you are glancing between meetings and want velocity, not just a static number. When one pillar is clearly the weakest and the score is in worrying territory, that card picks up a gentle warning accent so your eye lands in the right corner without us turning the whole dashboard red. The forward-looking watch area can show up to three concrete lines when the read supports it, stacked so secondary watches still read cleanly instead of hiding behind the first line.
entries.detail:
Behind the scenes the Pulse read is also nudged toward more specific language in the headline signals and the country snapshot — fewer vague "developments" lines, more names, numbers, and dates when the wires actually carry them. That is the same Pulse panel you already use; it should feel a little more opinionated and a little less samey across slow news days.

Sparklines and deltas depend on having a prior snapshot to compare against; the first load after a long absence may look quieter until the next refresh establishes a baseline.

entries.title: Home dashboard: saved articles in one place, quick jumps between topics, and a smarter article drawer
entries.summary: A bookmark icon in the header now opens a panel of everything you have saved with a star, shows how many you have at a glance, and lets you remove a story or tap straight back into the reader drawer without hunting the feed. When several topic sections are on the page, a slim row of pills appears so you can scroll straight to the block you care about instead of finger‑flicking past everything else. After a background news refresh, genuinely new headlines earn a short-lived "New" hint so fresh wires stand out without cluttering the page forever. Inside the article drawer, you can jump straight into headline search with one tap, or browse a few more stories from the same topic without closing the panel first.
entries.detail:
Saved articles still live on your device the way they always have — this release is about finding and revisiting them faster, not about syncing bookmarks to the cloud.

If the "New" ribbon disappears after about a minute and a half, that is intentional: it is meant as a gentle freshness signal, not a permanent label.

entries.title: Home dashboard: headlines stay fresher, and the PSX strip knows when the market is open
entries.summary: News on the home page now checks in with the server more often in the background, so new stories drift in without you hitting refresh, while the heavier economic datasets still refresh on the calmer schedule they always had. Timestamps on the breaking, briefing, and stock rows quietly update every minute so a "just now" label does not sit there until you reload. The PSX strip gained a small market-hours signal in plain language, a colour accent that hints how fresh the session data feels, and a "New" badge when a headline is truly hot off the wire. When the market is closed, the card says so in one line so you are not guessing why prices look quiet. If there are not enough PSX-tagged stories at that moment, the card can blend in carefully labelled economy and market coverage so the strip never looks half-empty.
entries.detail:
If you leave brief.pk hidden in another tab for a long stretch and come back, the page refetches news right away after a lengthy absence, and picks up a broader refresh when the whole dashboard has grown stale. That trades a little more background network chatter on active reading days for headlines that feel closer to live.

None of this changes billing, log-in, or paywall rules — it is how often the read surface refreshes and how honestly the PSX card explains its own freshness.

entries.title: brief's Pulse: tap a pillar to read the story without being thrown down the page
entries.summary: The six Pulse tiles on the home dashboard are still the quick scan of Pakistan across economy, security, politics, prices, diplomacy, and governance. Now, when you tap one, a focused panel slides open right beside the grid instead of jumping you halfway down the page to the category list. The panel keeps the score and trend, spells out the citizen impact in plain words, and threads in a handful of related headlines drawn from the live feed. When you are done, one tap takes you back to the full grid. The forward-looking watch line works like a button: it fills the search field so you can keep exploring in the intelligence panel. The headline-and-source line at the top of Pulse can expand to show which outlets dominate your current view so the numbers feel grounded rather than mysterious.
entries.detail:
Because the exploration stays inside the Pulse section, you lose less scroll position on long home sessions — especially on a phone where a sudden jump used to feel disorienting.

If you had been using Pulse purely as a shortcut into the category sections below, use search or your normal category navigation for that path; Pulse now prioritises explainability in place over teleporting the page.

entries.title: Header search feels steadier on phones — and clearer when something goes wrong
entries.summary: The intelligence search sheet on small screens now clears the thumb-height tab bar on installed phones instead of sliding underneath it. When the server cannot complete a search, you see a plain-language explanation instead of a cryptic error, and a Try again path that does not force a full page reload. Typing in the search box picks up a slightly faster rhythm: the query waits a beat after you pause, shows an honest loading state while it thinks, and always checks whatever you actually typed last so half-finished words do not send the wrong request.
entries.detail:
These are quality-of-life fixes for the same search experience you already had on the desktop layout; large screens keep their anchored panel behaviour.

If you still see an old, cramped sheet briefly after a deploy, one refresh usually picks up the latest layout files from the edge cache.

entries.title: Trackers and PSX News: find things faster, share exact links, and fresher headlines on the stocks page
entries.summary: The Constitution Tracker, Policy Tracker, and Accountability pages each grew a proper search box so you can narrow long lists without hunting by eye. On Constitution and Policy, opening an article or policy now updates the address bar — you can copy the URL, send it to a colleague, or bookmark it and land straight back on the same detail when you return. Constitution’s three audience tiles (legal, research, civic) now genuinely filter the registry instead of only scrolling it, with a clear highlight on whichever lens is active. Accountability’s table uses plain-language labels for where a row came from instead of internal jargon. On PSX News, the feed quietly refreshes on a steady rhythm while you keep the page open, picks up again when you return after the tab has been in the background a while, and your light-or-dark theme choice now sticks the same way it does on the home page.
entries.detail:
Search waits a fraction of a second after you stop typing so the page stays responsive on slower phones; Escape clears the box, and each page’s reset control clears it too. Shareable links use simple query patterns you can recognise in the address bar when a detail panel is open. None of this changes who can see paid sections or how your account works — it is navigation, filtering, and presentation.

If a link someone sends you does not open the right panel, ask them to copy the address again after the panel has fully opened, then refresh once. Cached copies of older JavaScript can occasionally lag a single deploy behind; a normal reload picks up the new behaviour.

entries.title: Phones: navigation that feels more like a real app
entries.summary: If you read brief.pk on a phone while signed in, the main sections are now one tap away on a bottom tab bar — Home, Economy, Policy, Account — with a More menu for Constitution, PSX News, and the rest. The top of the screen stays calm: just the brief.pk wordmark and the search box. The theme toggle, quick links, refresh, and sign-out sit in a slim strip above the tabs where your thumb naturally reaches, instead of crowding the header. The More menu itself got a clearer layout, a close control you can actually see, and softer grouping so extra destinations do not feel like an afterthought.
entries.detail: Nothing about your data or paywall status changed — this is navigation and presentation only. On a tablet or computer the experience is unchanged: the same links across the top you are used to. If anything looks off after a cached copy of the old layout, open the site once more or pull to refresh; the new chrome ships with the main web app automatically.

entries.title: PSX News: the feed now shows which rows can pin the ticker spotlight
entries.summary: On the dedicated PSX News page, some headlines looked like every row should open the company panel on the right — but index-wide market stories do not carry a single listable stock ticker in our dictionary, so there was nothing meaningful to pin. Rows that can spotlight a company now behave like buttons (pointer and hover), while index-wide rows stay visually quiet and include a short note telling you to use the blue headline link to read the story. Ticker chips that are only editorial tags (not dictionary symbols) appear as muted labels instead of fake buttons, and the page copy explains the difference up front.
entries.detail: The home-page PSX card was unchanged in this pass. If you mostly read KSE-100 session wraps, you will spend more time in the headline link; when a story names a bank, oil company, or other listed line, clicking the row or its ticker chip still opens the same live price, disclosures, and news archive on the right as before.

entries.title: Constitution Tracker: new editorial read on colonial legal continuity — with sources
entries.summary: The Constitution page now opens with a short editorial essay that explains, in plain language, how much of Pakistan’s everyday legal machinery still traces to Crown-era statutes and administration — and why that is framed as unfinished decolonisation rather than praise for old drafting. The piece includes a careful caveat around rough percentage shorthand used in legal circles, plus a numbered “Sources & further reading” section linking to an Oxford University Press constitutional history, the original Government of India Act 1935 as enacted in the UK statute archive, and an open-access Utah Law Review article on post-colonial common law and early constitutional breakdowns.
entries.detail: The registry of articles, amendments, and judgments below the essay is unchanged: it remains built from primary materials. The essay is labelled interpretive so readers can tell editorial voice from documentary entries.

entries.title: Fix: the close button on Constitution and Policy article trays is now actually visible
entries.summary: We shipped a fix earlier today that was supposed to keep the close X on Constitution Tracker and Policy Tracker article trays pinned to the top-right of the tray, even while you scroll. A paying customer noticed the X was still missing in practice. The reason: the tray was sliding in at the very top of the screen, which meant the top sixty pixels of the tray (the exact strip where the close X lives) ended up underneath the sticky top navigation header — directly behind the LOGOUT button. The X was rendered the whole time; it was just hidden. The tray now opens just below the header instead of behind it, so the close X is fully visible and clickable. Backdrop click-to-close and Escape continue to work as before.
entries.detail: Same fix applies on mobile: the header collapses to two rows there, so the tray on phones opens about a hundred pixels from the top to clear that taller header. The header pills remain visible above the tray so you can still navigate away to a different page without closing the article first.

entries.title: PSX news now stays on-topic, and three new feeds added for deeper equity coverage
entries.summary: A few of you flagged that the PSX news surface was occasionally surfacing stories that weren't about the stock market at all — most obviously cricket articles mentioning the HBL PSL, because HBL (Habib Bank) is the title sponsor and the same shorthand happens to be a real KSE-100 ticker. Same pattern occurred on a few textile-industry stories that mentioned 'PTC' without referring to Pakistan Tobacco Company. Both leaks are now closed. Separately, we added three dedicated equity-coverage feeds (KSE-100 sessions, PSX earnings and dividends, and a Business Recorder PSX scope), so corporate filings, dividend announcements, and market session wraps surface faster on the dashboard PSX card and on the dedicated PSX news page.
entries.detail:
What was going wrong. The PSX feed identifies stocks by looking for ticker symbols inside news headlines and descriptions. A bare ticker match is not specific enough on its own: HBL is both Habib Bank Limited (a real listed company) and the shorthand used by every Pakistani cricket outlet when they write about the HBL Pakistan Super League. PTC is both Pakistan Tobacco Company and the abbreviation used in textile-sector pieces referencing the Pakistan Textile Council. So cricket and textile stories were occasionally bleeding into the PSX surface.

What changed. A ticker mention in a headline must now also carry a financial signal somewhere in the story — either an event we already detect (earnings, dividend, M&A, listing, regulatory action) or one of a set of confirm words like profit, dividend, equity, share price, market cap, EPS, IPO, AGM, EGM, NCCPL, balance sheet, quarterly results, and similar. If a headline mentions HBL but the body is about cricket and there's no financial language anywhere, it doesn't get classified as PSX news. Real corporate news continues to flow through because journalism convention means earnings and dividend stories almost always carry at least one of those words.

Three new feeds. We also widened the ingestion pool with three additional sources focused specifically on equity coverage: a KSE-100 session feed, a PSX earnings and dividends feed, and a Business Recorder PSX feed. These ride the same 90-second cache pipeline as the existing feeds and start contributing immediately.

What to expect. Within roughly 90 seconds of this going live, the dashboard PSX card and the PSX news page begin pulling from a cleaner, denser pool. Cricket and textile-only stories should stop appearing; real corporate filings, dividend announcements, and KSE-100 session wraps should appear faster.

entries.title: Fix: the close button on Constitution and Policy article panels now stays visible while you scroll
entries.summary: If you opened an article on the Constitution Tracker or the Policy Tracker and read past the first screen, the close X scrolled out of view with the rest of the content. There was no obvious way to close the panel without pressing Escape on a keyboard. The close button now sits on the panel itself instead of inside the scrolling article, so it stays pinned to the top-right of the panel no matter how far you read. The dim area to the left of the panel is also now click-to-close, and Escape keeps working. Same release replaces one Constitution source citation (the Commission of Inquiry on Enforced Disappearances website) that was technically loading but showing internal WordPress plugin error messages on the page itself; it's been swapped for the Amnesty International Pakistan country report which covers the same evidence and loads cleanly.
entries.detail:
Why the close button was disappearing. Each article panel had its close button placed inside the scrolling content area at the top. The browser treated the X as part of the article body, so the moment you scrolled past the first viewport, the X went with everything else and you had nothing visible to click. The fix moves the X out of the scrolling element so it's attached to the panel chrome instead — it's now always at the top-right of the side panel, larger, and tinted red on hover so it reads as the primary close affordance.

While we were in there. Clicking on the dim area to the left of the panel now also closes it, matching how most modern side drawers work. Escape on a keyboard continues to close. When you open a different article without closing the previous one, the new article now starts at the top instead of inheriting the previous scroll position.

The broken source. Article 9 (Security of person) had evidence citing the Commission of Inquiry on Enforced Disappearances at coioed.pk. The site does respond — we re-probed every Constitution source URL programmatically this morning — but the body of the page is dominated by visible WordPress plugin error messages instead of the actual content. Replaced with the Amnesty International Pakistan country report, which is publicly accessible from any country and explicitly tracks enforced disappearances. The original government source was a Tier-1 official commission; the replacement is a Tier-2 NGO research report, and that source-provenance change is documented in the editorial notes attached to the Constitution data file. Every other Constitution source URL (the remaining 51 of 52) passed the audit.

entries.title: Fix: PSX News card on the dashboard now actually shows the headlines
entries.summary: The PSX News card on the home dashboard had a layout bug that made the headline text invisible — you'd see the ticker chips, the time, and the source name, but the actual headline between them was clipped out by the browser. Headlines are now visible (and given proper visual weight as the hero of each row), the rows are denser and easier to scan, and the card is taller so four to five stories fit without scrolling.
entries.detail:
What you'll see now. Each PSX news row reads top-to-bottom as: a chip strip for the tickers the story touches on the left with the time on the right, then a clear bold headline, then the source. The headline is bigger and gets a red highlight on hover so it's obvious where to click. The card itself fits a few more stories without forcing a scroll.

The bug. The previous card used a layout combination that, on certain widths, told the browser to clamp the headline to two lines AND to honour the parent column's width constraints — and the two instructions disagreed about how wide the headline was allowed to be. The browser resolved that disagreement by giving the headline zero width and hiding the text. Tickers, time, and source were on their own layout track and rendered fine, which is why the card looked structurally present but content-less.

The fix. The headline now sits in its own block element with an explicit full-width directive that defeats the collapse condition, plus the supporting CSS was rewritten to make the headline the visual hero of each row rather than equal-weight to the chrome around it. As a side benefit the row design is now closer to what a journalism card should look like.

entries.title: Header redesign: every page now shares the same navigation, and mobile gets a proper menu
entries.summary: Two things just got better. (1) Every primary page on brief.pk — the dashboard, Pakistan Economy, Policy Tracker, Constitution, and PSX News — now reads from one single source of truth for navigation, so any tab we add appears everywhere at once. No more 'why doesn't this link show up on page X' moments. (2) On phones and small screens, the cramped horizontal-scroll pill row has been replaced with a proper menu button (the three-line icon on the left of the brand). Tap it and a clean side drawer slides in with every section listed at a comfortable tap size.
entries.detail:
What changed for you on desktop. Nothing visible — the same five-pill nav bar across the top of every page. The change is structural: the nav is now generated from one configuration file instead of being hand-copied into each page's HTML. The next time we add a section (the constitution court tracker, a Punjab budget watch, whatever) it will appear on every page in the same release. The reason this matters is that PSX News quietly went live with the link missing from three pages last week, because someone had to remember to update five different files. That class of mistake is now structurally impossible.

What changed for you on mobile. The horizontal-scroll strip of nav pills below the brand is gone. In its place there's a single menu button (three horizontal lines) to the left of the brief.pk wordmark. Tap it and a panel slides in from the left of the screen with every section listed vertically at a generous tap size. Tap the section you want and the drawer closes and the page opens. Tap anywhere outside the drawer to dismiss. Press Escape on a Bluetooth keyboard to dismiss. The button animates from three lines into an X while the drawer is open so it's obvious where to tap to close it. The page underneath doesn't scroll while the drawer is open, so you won't lose your place.

Why we did it. Five sections in a horizontal pill row at the top of a phone screen already meant you couldn't see them all without scrolling — and most people don't realise the bar is scrollable horizontally. A proper menu button and a side drawer is the pattern every other mobile-first product uses for a reason: it's discoverable, it's roomy, and it scales as we add more sections without each one stealing a little more space from the next.

Nothing else changes. Same links, same destinations, same colours, same dark mode behaviour, same active-page highlight. This is plumbing made visible only where it makes the experience better.

entries.title: Fix: Constitution Tracker source links now all resolve from outside Pakistan
entries.summary: Several source citations on the Constitution Tracker pointed to documents on Pakistan government domains (National Assembly, Senate, education and research portals) that, while perfectly accessible from inside Pakistan, were geo-fenced or bot-blocked for readers browsing from elsewhere in the world. We've replaced every affected URL — across all 27 amendments, the 1973 Constitution full text, the implementation-gap evidence on Articles 25A, 29 and 38, and the historical-breach citations — with verified working alternatives. Every source link now resolves to the right page on the first click regardless of where you are reading from.
entries.detail:
The original Constitution Tracker shipped with primary sources pointing to the National Assembly's hosted PDFs for each amendment, plus a handful of research-organisation publication pages and a few news-archive links. Reader-side that meant: National Assembly PDFs returned a network error for anyone outside Pakistan; the Senate archive page worked but did not deep-link to anything specific; a couple of research portals (ASER, PIDE) blocked routine web requests; and four violation-source entries had been seeded with a placeholder dawn.com root URL that didn't go anywhere useful.

What we did. Every amendment from the 1st (1974) through the 21st (2015) now cites the pakistani.org annotated mirror, which has a dedicated page for each of those amendments and serves correctly from anywhere. The 22nd, 23rd, 24th and 25th amendments (2016–2018) — which pakistani.org has not yet mirrored individually — now cite the Dawn news article that documented each one's passage. The 1973 Constitution's full-text link now points to the pakistani.org consolidation rather than the National Assembly's geo-fenced PDF.

The contested 26th and 27th Amendments (October 2024 and November 2025) each get a richer set of citations than before — four 27th-Amendment-specific Dawn articles covering the Federal Constitutional Court structure explainer, the National Assembly passage, the Senate passage, and the presidential assent, plus a pakistani.org pointer to the new FCC chapter (Part VII, Chapter 1A) for primary text. Two 26th-Amendment-specific Dawn articles replace the previous senate.gov.pk pointer and a single Dawn URL that had been getting bot-challenged.

Implementation-gap evidence on the Fundamental Rights articles is now drawn from internationally-accessible sources where the previous source was bot-blocked: UNICEF Pakistan and the UNESCO Institute for Statistics for the out-of-school-children measure under the right-to-education article (Article 25A), the World Bank's Pakistan Gini indicator for the income-inequality measure under the principles-of-policy article (Article 38), and PILDAT for the legislative-monitoring measure under the freedom-of-association article (Article 29). The original Pakistani sources remain referenced in the underlying editorial notes; only the public link target changed.

Four breach entries — the 1969 abrogation under Yahya Khan, the 1988 dismissal of the Junejo government, the 1990 dismissal of the first Benazir government, and the 1993 Supreme Court restoration of the first Sharif government — had been carrying placeholder URLs to the Dawn homepage. They now cite specific Dawn historical-archive articles for each event, plus a pakistani.org pointer to the 1969 martial law proclamation alongside the Dawn coverage.

What we changed about how we work. For any future tracker page that is heavy on citations, we now require that every source URL be tested from at least one non-Pakistan vantage point before the page ships. That discipline note is now part of the Constitution Tracker's own editorial annotations and will be applied to the next data-heavy surface from the start.

Thank you to the reader who flagged this. The fix is live now.

entries.title: New: PSX News — a Pakistan-stock-focused news lane, built in response to your feedback
entries.summary: PSX News is a new section that pulls only news affecting Pakistan-listed companies — KSE-100 and KSE-30 names, with every headline tagged to the ticker and sector it touches. You'll find it as a card on the home dashboard right next to the Live Signal Matrix, and as a dedicated /psx-news subpage with sector and event-type filters, a ticker spotlight (live price + recent corporate disclosures + recent news on that company), and three audience lenses — investor, analyst, and civic — that pre-apply the filter combinations each one tends to want.
entries.detail:
Why we built this. A paying customer wrote in: 'I love the sorting of news as per subject such as Energy and Governance and Politics. Could you also add one that is purely PSX. Since Markets seems to be quite internationally broad. If we can have a dedicated PSX tab that allows us to see news especially local that affects PSX companies that would be great. Similar to what you have done with the live signal matrix but news focused.' The complaint was specific: the existing Markets category was being polluted by global stocks coverage, so the Pakistan-listed-company signal was getting drowned out. PSX News fixes that.

How the tagging works. We built a Pakistan Stock Exchange dictionary of large- and mid-cap names — the union of KSE-100 and KSE-30 — with each symbol mapped to its company name, its aliases (so 'Engro Corporation' matches ENGRO, 'Habib Bank' matches HBL, 'K-Electric' matches KEL), and its sector under PSX's own sectoral classification. Every article in our news pool gets scanned against the dictionary. If a ticker or alias matches with word-boundary discipline, the article is tagged with that ticker AND its sector AND any event types it carries (earnings, dividends, regulatory, M&A, macro, foreign flows, index changes). The category PSX is hoisted above the generic Markets bucket — so if a story matches a ticker, it never falls through into Markets.

What ships in this first release. (1) A home-page PSX News card, sibling of the Live Signal Matrix, showing the most recent PSX-tagged headlines with their ticker chips, source, and time. Clicking a ticker chip deep-links to the dedicated subpage filtered on that ticker. (2) A /psx-news subpage with: a hero showing the current KSE-100 level, today's PSX-tagged headline count, the top three mentioned tickers, and the dictionary size; three audience lens tiles that pre-apply event-type filters; a sector chip strip showing today's most-active sectors at a glance; a ticker search box; a two-pane feed where you click any headline (or any ticker chip) to spotlight that company on the right — with its live price, change percent, index membership badges, last seven corporate disclosures pulled from the PSX intel feed, and last seven recent news items on that ticker. (3) A 'N news' cross-link badge on every Live Signal Matrix row that has matching news, deep-linking back to the PSX News subpage filtered on that ticker. The two surfaces are designed to feel like one.

The taxonomy is PSX's. We use Pakistan Stock Exchange's own sectoral classification verbatim (e.g. COMMERCIAL BANKS, OIL & GAS EXPLORATION COMPANIES, POWER GENERATION & DISTRIBUTION). The display labels in the UI are friendlier ('Banking', 'Oil & Gas (E&P)', 'Power'), but the underlying authority is whatever PSX publishes. We don't invent sectors.

What's open. The dictionary at v0.1.0 captures large/mid-cap names that are stable across most quarterly KSE-100 reviews; small-cap rotation members may not be tagged. We'll regenerate the dictionary from the official PSX listings page on a recurring schedule in a near-term pass. The 'Suggest a ticker' flow lives in the methodology section of the subpage as a direct email link to the team for now — a formal feedback capture pipeline is parked while we keep handling these requests informally. Information, not investment advice — we classify, we don't recommend.

Thank you to the customer whose suggestion triggered this whole surface. We read every piece of feedback that comes in, and the ones that point at a real, specific gap get built fast.

entries.title: New: Pakistan Constitution Tracker — every article, amendment, breach, and anchor judgment since 1947, in one place
entries.summary: We've launched a new section on brief.pk dedicated to Pakistan's constitutional history — the 1956, 1962, and 1973 constitutions, every one of the 27 amendments to the 1973 Constitution (1974–2025) including the November 2025 amendment that created the new Federal Constitutional Court, eight documented constitutional breaches, and the nine Supreme Court judgments that have materially shaped what the text means in practice. Each record is sourced to a published primary document, dated, and cross-referenced. Three lenses — legal, research, civic — explain every entry so it works for the bar, the analyst, and the curious citizen.
entries.detail:
Why a separate tracker. The Policy Tracker covers what the government is doing this week. The Pakistan Economy page covers the macro story over 75 years. Neither of those handles the part of Pakistan's history that arguably matters most for understanding the rest of it: the constitution itself — what it says, what it was supposed to say at various points, when it was held in abeyance, and what the courts have done about it. That story lives in PDFs scattered across the National Assembly site, the Supreme Court archive, PIPS comparative editions, and a handful of academic monographs. We have pulled it into a single navigable surface.

What ships in this first release. Three founding documents — the 1956 Constitution (Pakistan's first), the 1962 Constitution (Ayub-era, presidential), and the 1973 Constitution (still in force, parliamentary). Twenty Articles of the 1973 Constitution treated in depth — including all the load-bearing Fundamental Rights articles (8, 9, 10, 10A, 19, 19A, 25, 25A), the Principles of Policy framework (29, 30, 38), the structural articles that have driven Pakistan's biggest constitutional debates (Article 6 on high treason, Article 2A on the Objectives Resolution, Article 58(2)(b) on presidential discretionary dissolution, Article 175A on judicial appointments, Article 184 on Supreme Court original jurisdiction, Article 199 on High Court writ jurisdiction, Article 232 on emergency, and Article 270AA on the validation of past unconstitutional acts).

All 27 amendments to the 1973 Constitution are tracked, from the first (1974, Bhutto-era) through the most recent — the 27th (November 2025, Shehbaz Sharif coalition) which created the new Federal Constitutional Court, repealed the Constitutional Benches of the Supreme Court and High Courts that the 26th Amendment had introduced only thirteen months earlier, and amended Article 243 so the Chief of Army Staff is now officially the Chief of Defence Forces and a Field Marshal for life. The list includes the two amendments that never made it into force (the 11th, drafted under the first Benazir government but lapsed, and the 15th, the Sharia-supremacy bill that passed the National Assembly but lapsed at the Senate). Each amendment carries its sponsor, date, plain-language summary, political context, and the articles it added, amended, or repealed. The 26th and 27th Amendments are read together as a single thirteen-month restructuring of Pakistan's apex judiciary — the most rapid such restructuring since the 1973 Constitution came into force.

Eight constitutional breaches are documented with the full chain — what happened, which judgment validated it, and which later judgment overturned it: the 1958 abrogation of the 1956 Constitution (validated by State v. Dosso, later overturned in spirit by Asma Jilani), the 1969 abrogation of the 1962 Constitution under Yahya Khan, the 1977 PCO under Zia-ul-Haq (validated by Begum Nusrat Bhutto), the four uses of Article 58(2)(b) to dismiss elected governments (Junejo 1988, Bhutto 1990, Sharif 1993, Bhutto 1996), the 1999 Musharraf takeover (validated by Zafar Ali Shah), and the 3 November 2007 emergency under Musharraf (struck down by Sindh High Court Bar Association v. Federation in 2009).

Nine anchor Supreme Court judgments are summarised in 200 words each and cross-linked to the breaches they validated or overturned, the amendments they bore on, and the articles they materially interpreted. Each carries its PLD citation and (where available) a link out to the Supreme Court archive PDF.

The implementation-gap view. For each Fundamental Right with documented evidence, we show the law-on-paper next to the state-in-practice — citing ASER for the right-to-education gap under Article 25A (about 22.8 million out-of-school children), HRCP and the Commission of Inquiry on Enforced Disappearances for Article 9, Freedom Network and HRCP for Article 19 press-freedom restrictions, CPDI and PILDAT for Article 19A right-to-information implementation, and PILDAT and FAFEN for the annual reporting obligation under Article 29(3) that is widely treated as a dead letter. Every evidence entry is dated and typed (audit, survey, official report, etc.).

The framing rule. Constitutional history is contested in Pakistan in ways that matter. The page does not adjudicate — it describes what observers documented, attributing every characterisation to the actor that made it. The Bhutto trial and execution, the 1977 PCO, the 1999 takeover, the 17th Amendment political bargain, the contested 26th Amendment of October 2024, and the contested 27th Amendment of November 2025 (which created a separate Federal Constitutional Court atop the Supreme Court) are each treated under the framing rule so readers see the factual sequence and the dissenting voices, not an editorial verdict.

What's open. The article-by-article comparison tool that diffs the 1956, 1962, and 1973 constitutions is a Phase 2 build. Article-by-article deep coverage of the new Federal Constitutional Court chapter (Part VII, Chapter 1A; Articles 175B–175L) added by the 27th Amendment is also Phase 2 — the amendment card explains the structural shift, but each new article gets its own dedicated treatment in the next pass once the National Assembly publishes a post-27th-Amendment consolidated PDF. Verbatim full-text quotation of six structural articles (Articles 58(2)(b), 175A, 184, 199, 232, and 270AA) is deferred to that same byte-for-byte cross-check pass, for the same reason. Plain-language summary and political background for those articles ship in this release. An automated ingestion pipeline that watches the NA archive, the Senate ordinances feed, and the Supreme Court judgments archive for new entries lands in Phase 5. This first release is the editorial spine; everything else builds on it.

Open sources only. The National Assembly archive, the Senate amendment ordinance PDFs, the Supreme Court judgments archive, PIPS comparative editions, HRCP / PILDAT / ASER / FAFEN / Centre for Peace and Development Initiatives / Pakistan Bureau of Statistics for implementation-gap evidence, and the Dawn / Daily Times / The News archives for contemporaneous newspaper-of-record citations on contested events. No paid feeds. Every source carries a retrieved-on date.

entries.title: Policy Tracker — provincial coverage is live (Punjab, Sindh, KP, Balochistan, ICT, AJK, Gilgit-Baltistan)
entries.summary: The Pakistan Policy Tracker now covers policy across all four provinces, Islamabad Capital Territory, and Pakistan's two autonomous territories (Azad Jammu & Kashmir and Gilgit-Baltistan) — not just federal regulators and the IMF programme. Sixteen new sub-federal records are tracked, the registry grew from 12 to 28 policies, and the page picked up a new Jurisdiction filter so you can narrow the view to a single province or any combination you care about.
entries.detail:
Until today the tracker was federal-only — IMF, SBP, FBR, NEPRA, FATF, NDC, MoITT, MoCC. That misses most of what actually governs Pakistanis day to day, because the 18th Amendment moved a lot of the rulebook (sales tax on services, education, health, environment, agriculture, mining, urban planning, local government) to the provinces. The first sub-federal wave goes live in this release.

What's added:

• Punjab (3 records) — PRA Sales Tax on Services Act 2012, the Punjab FY25–26 budget, and the Punjab Information Technology Board (PITB) framework that underpins citizen-facing digital services across the province.

• Sindh (3) — SRB Sales Tax on Services Act 2011, the Sindh FY25–26 budget, and the Sindh Environmental Protection Act 2014 that gates industrial and infrastructure approvals in Karachi and the rest of the province.

• Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (3) — the KP Revenue Authority's sales-tax-on-services regime, the KP FY25–26 budget (including the Sehat Card Plus envelope and the merged-districts development line), and the KP Mines & Minerals Act 2017 that governs the province's chromite, copper, marble and coal concessions.

• Balochistan (2) — the BRA Sales Tax on Services Act 2015 and the Balochistan FY25–26 budget.

• Islamabad Capital Territory (1) — the CDA master-plan and zoning framework that controls Islamabad's land use, commercial conversions, and Margalla Hills enforcement.

• Azad Jammu & Kashmir (2) — the AJK FY25–26 budget and the AJK Interim Constitution Act 1974 (as amended in 2018), which is the foundational legal framework governing AJK's relationship with the federation.

• Gilgit-Baltistan (2) — the GB FY25–26 budget and the Gilgit-Baltistan Order 2018, which replaced the 2009 Self-Governance Order and remains the live legal anchor underneath the recurring provisional-province debate.

Every record carries the editor-curated trust block that the rest of the tracker uses: a plain-English summary, a one-paragraph impact note, audience-specific commentary for investors, researchers and policy professionals, a dated change log, and at least one canonical source URL (with an archive fallback link beside it if the source ever goes offline or geo-blocks you).

What's new on the page:

Every card and detail panel now shows a jurisdiction pill in the same row as the domain — Federal, Punjab, Sindh, KP, BAL, ICT, AJK, or GB — colour-coded so you can scan a long list quickly. Federal records get a softer treatment so they don't drown out the rest.

A new "Jurisdiction" filter row sits on the filter bar with one chip per jurisdiction plus an "All jurisdictions" reset. Pick Punjab and Sindh and the registry narrows to just those two; clear it and you're back to everything. The filter is stored in the URL (?jurisdictions=punjab,ajk for example) so a link or bookmark reproduces the view. Jurisdiction works alongside the existing Domain and Status filters and the three audience presets (Investor, Researcher, Policy Pro) — picking a jurisdiction doesn't reset your audience preset.

What hasn't changed: every existing federal record continues to render and filter exactly as before. The data lives in the same registry file the page has always read from, every new source URL has been retrieved and dated, and the API contract underneath the page is unchanged. This is purely additive coverage plus the surface to make it usable.

entries.title: Pakistan Economy page — IMF Outlook 2025–2031 section is back, now on the April 2026 WEO
entries.summary: The IMF Outlook section on the Pakistan Economy page (six KPI tiles, six mini-charts, and the forecast verdict) was rendering empty. It now shows the latest IMF World Economic Outlook (April 2026) figures for Pakistan: real GDP growth, CPI inflation, government debt, current-account balance, GDP per capita PPP, and unemployment — with the forecast years out to 2031 drawn as dashed lines on each sparkline.
entries.detail:
Last week's automated refresh of IMF data didn't make it through; the section ended up holding empty arrays instead of forecasts, which is why every tile and chart in that block looked blank. This release re-runs the IMF data fetch against the latest WEO vintage and ships the populated payload alongside the page.

The source note under the section now correctly reads "April 2026" — the vintage of the figures actually on screen. Forecast lines (2026 onward) continue to be drawn dashed to make the actual-vs-projected boundary obvious, and the headline KPI tiles include a small up / down / unchanged indicator comparing each first-forecast-year value against the latest historical year.

While we were in there, we also locked the two long-horizon comparison charts above ("The Long Decline" and "The Great Divergence") to their World Bank sources explicitly, so they continue to show the full 1947–today story regardless of how the IMF dataset evolves week to week. No change to those charts is visible — this is a safety net to prevent a silent regression as the IMF data flow comes back online.

entries.title: Pakistan Economy page — three charts now render correctly on first load
entries.summary: Three charts on the Pakistan Economy page were rendering as empty boxes on first load: "The Long Decline" (GDP growth 1947 → today), "The Divergence" (Pakistan vs Bangladesh, India, Vietnam, Sri Lanka GDP per capita since 1971), and "The Great Divergence" (Pakistan vs India vs Bangladesh indexed to 100 at 1947). All three now draw correctly the moment the page loads — no clicking required.
entries.detail:
The Long Decline and Great Divergence charts were silently bailing out before drawing because of a stale data path that pointed at an empty source first instead of falling through to the World Bank data that actually has the numbers. The Divergence peer chart was rendering its axes and grid but skipping every country line on first paint — clicking a country toggle below the chart made each one appear, but the default state on page load was a blank chart with axis labels and nothing else.

All three are fixed in this release. On a fresh load of the Pakistan Economy page you should now see: a full 79-year GDP growth line with the political-event markers, IMF programme dashes and military / civilian / IMF-era background shading on The Long Decline; all five country lines drawn on The Divergence (Pakistan, Bangladesh, India, Vietnam, Sri Lanka) along with the "BGD overtakes PAK" marker and the read-out beneath the chart; and the indexed 1947-to-today comparison for Pakistan, India, and Bangladesh on The Great Divergence with the parity baseline at 100.

No data changed — only the rendering. If you had previously bookmarked or refreshed the page and only ever saw empty chart boxes for these three sections, a hard reload will pick up the fix.

entries.title: Policy Tracker now covers climate — nine domains, real records on every one
entries.summary: The Policy Tracker grew a ninth domain: climate. Four real policies are live on day one — Pakistan's Updated NDC 2021, the National Climate Change Policy 2021, the Living Indus Initiative, and the Carbon Market Policy 2024 draft. Each one carries the same sourcing, dates, key milestones, and audience-specific impact notes you already see on every other policy in the registry.
entries.detail:
Climate policy was the most glaring gap in the original tracker. Pakistan's climate exposure is structural — the 2022 super-floods, the Indus basin's water stress, and the country's outsized share of global climate-finance asks all sit upstream of investment, fiscal, and energy decisions. Leaving climate uncovered made the tracker incomplete in a way that mattered.

This release adds the climate domain end-to-end. Climate gets its own filter chip on the registry, its own accent color (a deep green that's distinct from the existing eight), and its own live news lane match rules so headlines about the NDC, MoCC, Article 6 carbon credits, Living Indus, and Pakistan's climate-finance pledges now surface against the right policy record automatically.

The four day-one records are: (1) Pakistan's Updated Nationally Determined Contribution submitted to UNFCCC in October 2021, with its 50% emission-reduction target and 60% renewable electricity goal by 2030; (2) the National Climate Change Policy 2021, the federal framework that operationalizes climate-screening of public investments; (3) the Living Indus Initiative, the government's umbrella programme for Indus Basin climate resilience launched after the 2022 floods; and (4) the Carbon Market Policy 2024 draft, which would let Pakistan formally trade carbon credits under Paris Agreement Article 6.

The investor preset on the Policy Tracker now defaults to including climate alongside fiscal, monetary, trade, and energy — because climate-screening at the federal investment-approval stage and the renewable electricity ceiling on new fossil generation are first-order facts for anyone building or financing in Pakistan today.

entries.title: One header, three pages — Dashboard, Pakistan Economy, and Policy Tracker are now one click apart
entries.summary: Every signed-in page now shows the same navigation strip at the top: Dashboard · Pakistan Economy · Policy Tracker. Wherever you are, the same three pills tell you what exists and the active one tells you where you are. The brand logo on every page is now also a clickable shortcut back to the dashboard.
entries.detail:
Until tonight, the dashboard didn't advertise that Pakistan Economy or the Policy Tracker existed at all — you had to know the URL. The two subpages had a navigation strip, but they were the only ones. Three pages, three different header treatments.

That's fixed. The dashboard now carries the same three-pill nav as the subpages, with Dashboard highlighted as the active page. The visual language (red active pill, white inactive pill, hover state) is identical across all three pages so the muscle memory transfers. The brief.pk wordmark, top-left, is now a link back to the dashboard from anywhere in the app.

Mobile readers see the nav as a horizontally-scrollable strip directly under the brand, with tap targets sized for fingers (≥36px tall). On narrower phones the strip tightens up automatically. On desktop the three pills sit inline with a thin dot separator between them.

We also brought the light/dark theme toggle to the dashboard — it was previously only on the subpages, which felt inconsistent. Click the sun/moon icon in the header on any page; your choice persists across pages and reloads.

entries.title: Policy Tracker: news lane goes live and the page now reads from two registry layers
entries.summary: The Policy Tracker's "Live news lane" is now actually live. Headlines from the news feed (Dawn, The News, Business Recorder, Reuters, FT, Bloomberg, and 20+ more outlets) are matched in real time to each tracked policy, so when something moves on a rule we're following you see the coverage on the same page as the rule itself.
entries.detail:
When we launched the Policy Tracker earlier today, the news lane was a placeholder — each policy showed which keywords we'd be watching for. That placeholder is gone. The lane now pulls live from the same news feed that powers the dashboard, runs each policy's keyword set against every recent headline, and surfaces the most recent matches next to the policy they touch. Click through goes straight to the original outlet. We don't summarize, paraphrase, or rerank by tone.

Underneath the page, the registry now reads from two layers instead of one. The first is the editor-curated fixture you already saw: eight cited records across the eight domains, written by us. The second is an automated layer produced by our ingestion pipeline. Today the automated layer contributes mostly transparency (which official sources our pipeline reached, what it found) — it doesn't add records to the registry yet. As we wire each adapter and stand up the structured-extraction step, those records will start flowing into the auto layer. The editor-curated layer always wins on conflict, so the records you trust today will not get overwritten by anything machine-derived. Records the pipeline does contribute will carry a small "Auto-ingested" badge so you can always tell what came from where.

A daily scheduled run now refreshes the auto layer at 08:00 PKT. Anything the pipeline cannot reach is logged in the file itself; no broken sources hide silently.

For what's still ahead: the IMF press-release feed needs rediscovery (their RSS endpoint was retired and we won't ship brittle scraping), the State Bank's monetary-policy index needs a stable source endpoint, and the structured-extraction step for less-structured documents (FBR notifications, NEPRA tariffs, SECP circulars) requires a separate decision on the LLM provider we'll use. Those land in subsequent Phase 2 releases without disrupting anything you see today.

entries.title: Pakistan Policy Tracker: every government rule that matters, in one place
entries.summary: We added a live registry of Pakistan's policy moves across eight domains—fiscal, monetary, trade, energy, regulatory, social, foreign, and security. Each entry is sourced, dated, and traceable back to an original SBP statement, FBR notification, NEPRA determination, IMF release, or World Bank indicator. The page sits inside the signed-in research hub alongside GDP History.
entries.detail:
Pakistan's policy environment moves fast and across many regulators. Following the State Bank's monetary stance, NEPRA tariff decisions, FBR notifications, the latest IMF review, and BISP coverage usually means watching half a dozen websites and trying to remember what each agency last announced. The Policy Tracker collects those moves into one filterable view so you can read across them rather than chasing each in isolation.

Three audience presets shape the page differently. Investors and operators see fiscal, monetary, and trade policy first, with currency and energy decisions surfaced as recent moves. Researchers and journalists get a registry-first view with sourcing and dates emphasized. Policy professionals see the full domain matrix with status flags for live, draft, pending, and amended rules.

Every record links back to the original document we built the entry from—an SBP statement, an FBR SRO, a NEPRA determination, an IMF press release, or a World Bank indicator series. We refuse to cite anything we cannot point you to. A methodology panel on the page explains how we pick what to track and how we summarize.

This is a Phase 1 release with eight curated records seeded across seven of the eight domains; trade will populate as current actions are announced. A more automated ingestion pipeline is in flight and will gradually widen coverage without changing the page experience for readers.

entries.title: Sign-in restored and protected by a new automated check
entries.summary: Sign-in is working again on every page. A recent layout change accidentally removed a tiny element the sign-in page expected to find, which silently stopped the Google button and the magic-link email form from responding. Accounts were never at risk—the page just couldn’t wire itself up.
entries.detail:
We patched the page to tolerate the missing element so the same shape of bug cannot block sign-in again.

Beyond the patch, we added a dedicated automated check to the status pipeline that opens a real incident the moment any required element on the sign-in (or paid-access) page goes missing, or any inline script tries to use an element that is not in the page. That means the public status page will now reflect this class of regression instead of staying green while users are stuck.

Thank you to everyone who noticed and reported it.

entries.title: Release notes: major milestones and fuller explanations
entries.summary: The public release-notes page now opens with major product chapters spelled out in depth. Weekly updates still appear below, but each item shows the full story—what changed, why it matters, and how you might notice it when using brief.pk.
entries.detail:
You should not need an engineering background to understand this page. “Major milestones” are the big arcs of the product (for example the core dashboard, GDP History, and intelligence search). Everything else is grouped by week, and every note is written so you can read it start to finish.

We still do not show raw commit history or internal ticket numbers here—the goal is trust and clarity for readers, not a developer log.

entries.title: One consistent footer across the public site and status pages
entries.summary: Legal links, brand context, research shortcuts, and the aggregated-intelligence credit line now follow the same layout everywhere. If you are looking for Privacy, Terms, GDP History, or how to reach the team, the footer behaves predictably no matter which page you landed on.
entries.detail: Small inconsistencies—different footers on different templates—make a product feel unmaintained even when the underlying data is solid. Aligning footers reduces that friction and makes compliance pages easier to find under stress.

entries.title: Less sensitive operational detail on public status surfaces
entries.summary: We reviewed what the public status site should reveal about internal routing and providers. Anything that was helpful only to operators—not to readers—was removed or generalized so the surface stays trustworthy without becoming a map of our stack.
entries.detail: Security improvements are not always visible features. This change is about reducing accidental disclosure while keeping honest health reporting. If you rely on the status page, you should still see whether core endpoints respond—but not internal configuration trivia.

entries.title: Status site custom domain
entries.summary: Traffic for the health dashboard is served from our dedicated status domain so bookmarks, incident posts, and support emails can point to one stable URL.
entries.detail: Custom domains on GitHub Pages require careful DNS setup. Once in place, readers see a URL that matches the rest of the brief.pk ecosystem.

entries.title: Clearer status wording for sign-in paths
entries.summary: Login health rows on the status site use plainer language so you can tell whether magic link, Google, and the login page themselves are reachable—without jargon about implementation.
entries.detail: ‘Reachable’ versus ‘configured’ can confuse people during an incident. Copy now matches how readers actually think about logging in.

entries.title: Login paths tracked alongside core APIs
entries.summary: Magic link, Google OAuth, and the login page itself appear next to news, markets, and other components so you can see account access health in the same glance as content feeds.
entries.detail: Users blame ‘the site’ when sign-in fails—even if articles still load. Elevating login checks makes incidents easier to diagnose and communicate.

entries.title: More resilient login health during partial rollouts
entries.summary: When health payloads are mid-deploy, the status UI now degrades gracefully instead of flashing alarming states that do not match what end users experience.
entries.detail: Synthetic checks sometimes race ahead of production config. Fallbacks keep the dashboard honest: show uncertainty explicitly rather than guessing red.

entries.title: Mobile layout: easier reading on phones
entries.summary: Spacing, safe areas, scrollable tables, and card wrapping were tuned so the dashboard remains usable on small screens—especially when markets move and you are not at a desk.
entries.detail: Pakistan-heavy audiences often reach for a phone first. These refinements reduce pinch-zooming and sideways scrolling on common breakpoints without changing the underlying data.

entries.title: Safer rendering when payloads are incomplete
entries.summary: If a probe returns partial JSON, panels skip missing fields instead of blanking the whole page—especially important during deploy windows.
entries.detail: Defensive coding here protects readers from transient infrastructure hiccups that are not ‘outages’ in the user-facing sense.

entries.title: Status dashboard redesign
entries.summary: The public status page now mirrors how engineers think about uptime—component grid, incident banner, and per-service bars—but stays readable for non-technical stakeholders.
entries.detail: The goal was transparency without noise. Colour, typography, and spacing follow the same brand system as the main product so the two sites feel related.

entries.title: Search endpoint in monitoring docs
entries.summary: Runbooks and status expectations now treat merged search the same as core news: one more step toward consistent on-call coverage.
entries.detail: Documentation drifts fast on small teams. This ties observability words to what actually ships in production.

entries.title: Lighter visual theme for the status site
entries.summary: Higher contrast hierarchy and calmer backgrounds make long incident days easier on the eyes—especially on laptop screens.
entries.detail: Pure aesthetics sometimes support comprehension: when hierarchy is obvious, people parse severity faster.

entries.title: Scheduled health snapshots (ongoing)
entries.summary: Automation continues to commit fresh status snapshots on a regular cadence so charts and JSON history stay current without manual babysitting.
entries.detail: Readers rarely notice this work directly, but it keeps timelines honest when you scroll backward through incidents.

entries.title: Deeper synthetic analytics in status tooling
entries.summary: Operator-facing panels gained journey-style signals and richer component analytics so we can see behaviour across probes—not just one green/red boolean per route.
entries.detail: This primarily helps the team maintain reliability; readers still see the same high-level health story, with more accuracy behind the scenes.

entries.title: Public status site on GitHub Pages
entries.summary: We published an always-on status dashboard with automated checks, historical JSON artifacts, and a layout aligned with brief.pk branding.
entries.detail: External status pages build trust: when news or markets hiccup, a neutral health surface reduces rumor and duplicate reports.

entries.title: Intelligence Search and a richer Pulse experience
entries.summary: Header search now treats the whole Pakistan news corpus as one intelligence surface: the same merged feeds that power the main dashboard, plus fresh results when you need them, sorted with newest stories first. Pulse continues to explain “what matters now” in plain language for readers who want context without digging through dozens of headlines.
entries.detail:
If you follow Pakistan closely, you often care about both speed (“what just broke?”) and recall (“what did we already know?”). Intelligence Search is designed for that dual need. When you type a query, results draw from the same article pool as the live news experience so your search, Pulse, and the main feed stay aligned instead of contradicting each other.

Results you see can come from the core merged feed or from a short-lived supplemental lookup when the topic needs fresher headlines. The interface shows where each result came from so you can judge how to use it. We also added habits power users expect: recent queries, chips that relate to what Pulse is watching, shareable URLs with your search text, and keyboard shortcuts so you can move quickly without hunting for tiny controls.

Pulse sits alongside search on purpose—it is the “executive summary” layer. It aims to answer what is happening, what may unfold soon, and how confident the read is, without asking you to interpret raw model output. Together, search plus Pulse make brief.pk feel closer to a single workspace than a static headline page.

entries.title: Pakistan GDP History: decades of context in one research-grade view
entries.summary: We shipped a dedicated GDP History experience that stretches from independence through today, layers in multilateral outlooks where appropriate, and places economic turning points next to governance and crisis context so you can see why the curve moved—not just the numbers.
entries.detail:
Macro statistics on their own are easy to misread. A GDP print or revision can look small on a chart but mean a great deal for debt, inflation, or reform timing. The GDP History module was built for readers who want the long arc: growth episodes, sharp contractions, recoveries, and how Pakistan compares to its own past.

The view combines long-run historical estimates with more recent official and multilateral series, and pairs the chart with narrative and markers so a student, journalist, or diaspora investor can understand what changed in each era. The intent is not to replace formal national accounts work—it is to give credible, well-sourced context in one place.

If you only use brief.pk for news and markets, think of GDP History as the “slow journalism” companion: fewer headlines, more framing, and numbers you can cite with confidence because the sources are documented on the page.

entries.title: The Pakistan Intelligence Dashboard: news, markets, map, and research in one hub
entries.summary: brief.pk launched as a single authenticated workspace for Pakistan-focused intelligence—curated news, market snapshot (including USD/PKR and PSX context), city-level map telemetry, macro and security-economy research modules, and admin-backed controls so the product can evolve without fragile hard-coding.
entries.detail:
Most readers bounce between a news app, a broker terminal, a weather or AQI site, and scattered PDFs for macro data. The core dashboard was an explicit bet that Pakistan watchers need one reliable home screen: fast headlines when something is moving, market context when FX or equities lead the story, and research panels when the narrative is really about inflation, reserves, reform, or stability.

Auth (magic link and Google) keeps the experience tailored while protecting heavier features. The admin console lets operators enable or pause sections responsibly as sources and costs change. That foundation is what later made Intelligence Search, GDP History, and deeper AI interpretation possible without splintering into unrelated micro-sites.

If you are new to brief.pk, start with the main dashboard: live news, Pulse when it is enabled for your account, markets strip, and the map. Everything else is organized as modules so you can go deep when you need to—not before.
